evidence: High-import central contracts/facades are concentrated in a few thin files (`src/domains/generation/types/generationViewRow.ts`, `src/integrations/supabase/client.ts`, `src/shared/lib/errorHandling/handleError.ts`) that mainly alias or compose existing shapes/APIs, creating broad ownership choke points.

`src/domains/generation/types/generationViewRow.ts` mixes persisted, derived, and optimistic UI concerns into a shared exported contract (`GenerationRow`, `GenerationUiRow`) that is then reused by `src/domains/generation/types/shotViewRow.ts`, expanding ripple radius for type evolution.
No clear import-time side effects were found in the assigned files; modules are mostly declarative exports/components/hooks.

`generationTaskRepository.parsePrimaryTaskId` treats non-array `tasks` as `invalid_tasks_shape`, while `generationTransformers.extractTaskId` still accepts legacy string `tasks`, creating divergent boundary behavior for the same field.

`generationTaskCache.preloadGenerationTaskMappings` only prefetches task rows when mapping status is `ok`, so the stricter parser can suppress downstream preloading even when a usable task id exists in legacy string form.

`useGenerationInvalidation` scheduled invalidation path executes `void executeQueryLifecycleActions(...)` inside `setTimeout` without error handling, while other async seam code (for example `adjacentPagePreloadCoordinator.prefetchPage`) explicitly catches and routes errors, producing inconsistent cross-module lifecycle/error contracts.

Join-clips metadata boundaries use three different failure contracts for similar duration-fetch operations: sentinel `0` (`clipInitService.getVideoDurationFromUrl`), `OperationResult` (`loadClipDurationResult`), and thrown exception (`loadClipDuration`), increasing orchestration/test seam complexity.

findings.summary: Generation view types blend persistence, derived view data, and optimistic UI overlays in one shared contract, weakening boundary ownership.
evidence: `GenerationRow` is defined as `PersistedGenerationRow & GenerationDerivedFields`, while `GenerationUiRow` adds optimistic UI overlay fields in the same module.
`ShotViewModel.images` depends on `GenerationRow`, so shot-domain contracts inherit generation-view coupling and any future field-layer changes ripple through both domains.
findings.suggestion: Split contracts by boundary: keep persisted/domain types separate from UI overlay types, and introduce explicit mapper-owned view DTOs for cross-domain sharing so shot contracts depend on stable domain-facing shapes only.

findings.summary: Highly imported global facades are mostly pass-through aliases, concentrating coupling without adding strong architectural separation.
evidence: `handleError` is a back-compat alias that forwards directly to `reportRuntimeError` with no additional policy.
`getSupabaseClient` is exported as a renamed alias of `getSupabaseRuntimeClient`, and initialization is re-exported unchanged.
`shared/hooks/mobile/index.ts` is a pure barrel export; combined with high importer counts, these files become central dependency hubs where naming/back-compat decisions affect many callers.
findings.suggestion: Time-box deprecation aliases and migrate callers to canonical APIs; keep only one stable entrypoint per concern and remove redundant compatibility layers once migration completes.

findings.summary: Task-id boundary parsing is inconsistent across generation modules.
evidence: `parsePrimaryTaskId` returns `invalid_tasks_shape` for any non-array `tasks` value.
`extractTaskId` in transformers accepts both `string[]` and single `string` task values.
Task cache preloading requires mapping status `ok`, so records with legacy string task shape can be treated as invalid and skipped.
findings.suggestion: Unify task-id decoding into a shared helper used by repository and transformers, accepting both current and legacy shapes; keep mapping status semantics aligned so cache preloading and UI transforms make the same decision for identical rows.

findings.summary: Scheduled invalidation drops query lifecycle errors at the async seam.
evidence: `scheduleInvalidationPlan` runs `void executeQueryLifecycleActions(...)` inside a timeout without `try/catch` or reporting.
Immediate invalidation paths are awaited and can surface failures, but scheduled paths silently ignore them.
`prefetchPage` in adjacent preloading catches and forwards errors via `onError`, showing a different boundary contract for similar async query orchestration.
findings.suggestion: Wrap scheduled execution in `try/catch` and route failures through a consistent reporting callback (or shared error facade) so delayed and immediate invalidation flows expose comparable error/lifecycle behavior.

findings.summary: Clip duration APIs expose conflicting failure semantics across join-clips seams.
evidence: `getVideoDurationFromUrl` resolves to `0` on error and caller proceeds with clip insertion.
`loadClipDurationResult` returns structured `OperationResult` failure metadata.
`loadClipDuration` converts that failure into a thrown error, creating a third consumer-facing contract.
findings.suggestion: Standardize duration-fetch boundary behavior to one contract (preferably `OperationResult` at seam level), then adapt once at UI entrypoints; avoid mixing sentinel values and exceptions for the same operation type.
